sections.heading: What is an Activity?
sections.content: An Activity is one of the core building blocks of an Android app. It represents a single screen with a user interface — for example, a login screen, a home feed, or a settings page. Every screen the user sees is typically backed by an Activity (or a Fragment inside one). Activities are managed by the Android system, which creates, pauses, resumes, and destroys them based on user actions and system conditions.

sections.heading: Key Characteristics of an Activity
items: Represents one screen of your app with a visual UI
Must be declared in AndroidManifest.xml to be recognized by the system
Extends the AppCompatActivity (or ComponentActivity) base class
Has a defined lifecycle managed entirely by the Android OS
Communicates with other Activities using Intents
Can host multiple Fragments to build complex UIs
Has its own back stack entry — pressing Back destroys or pauses the current Activity
Can save and restore its UI state across configuration changes (e.g., screen rotation)

sections.heading: What is the Activity Lifecycle?
sections.content: The Activity lifecycle is a set of states an Activity passes through from the moment it is created to the moment it is destroyed. Android calls specific callback methods at each transition so your app can respond correctly — for example, pausing media playback when the app goes to the background, or saving data before the Activity is destroyed. Understanding and implementing these callbacks correctly is essential to building stable, efficient Android apps.

rows.typical_use: Initialize UI, set content view, set up ViewModel, bind views

rows.called_when: Activity becomes visible to the user
rows.typical_use: Start animations or UI updates that should be visible

rows.called_when: Activity is in the foreground and interactive
rows.typical_use: Start camera, sensors, or resume media playback

rows.called_when: Activity is partially obscured or losing focus
rows.typical_use: Pause camera, sensors, animations, or ongoing playback

rows.called_when: Activity is completely hidden from the user
rows.typical_use: Release heavy resources, stop network calls, save draft data

rows.called_when: Activity is returning to the foreground after being stopped

rows.typical_use: Release all remaining resources, cancel coroutines, clean up

sections.heading: The Lifecycle Flow Step by Step
items: App launched → onCreate() → onStart() → onResume() [Activity is running]
User presses Home → onPause() → onStop() [Activity is in background]
User returns to app → onRestart() → onStart() → onResume() [Activity is running again]
Another Activity opens on top → onPause() [partially visible] → onStop() [fully hidden]
User presses Back → onPause() → onStop() → onDestroy() [Activity is finished]
System kills app due to low memory → onPause() → onStop() → (process killed)
Screen rotated → onPause() → onStop() → onDestroy() → onCreate() → onStart() → onResume()

sections.heading: onCreate() — The Most Important Callback
sections.content: onCreate() is called once when the Activity is first created. This is where you do all one-time initialization: calling setContentView() to inflate your layout, initializing your ViewModel, setting up RecyclerView adapters, and attaching click listeners. It receives a savedInstanceState Bundle that contains previously saved state data if the Activity is being recreated after a configuration change or system kill.

sections.heading: What to Do in Each Lifecycle Method
items: onCreate() – setContentView(), ViewModel init, click listeners, RecyclerView setup, observe LiveData
onStart() – Register listeners that need to be active while the Activity is visible
onResume() – Start camera preview, resume audio/video playback, re-register sensors
onPause() – Pause playback, release camera, stop sensor updates (keep it fast — no heavy work)
onStop() – Save user data drafts, stop background tasks, release network connections
onRestart() – Refresh data that may have changed while the Activity was stopped
onDestroy() – Release all remaining resources, cancel all coroutines and observers

sections.heading: Saving & Restoring Instance State
sections.content: When the system destroys and recreates an Activity (e.g., on screen rotation), it gives you a chance to save temporary UI state using onSaveInstanceState(). The saved Bundle is then passed back to onCreate() and onRestoreInstanceState() so you can restore the UI exactly as it was. For more complex or persistent state, use ViewModel which survives configuration changes automatically.

rows.onsaveinstancestate: Save small, transient UI state (e.g., scroll position, text input)
rows.viewmodel: Hold and survive UI-related data across configuration changes

sections.heading: Configuration Changes & Activity Recreation
items: Rotating the screen is the most common configuration change that triggers Activity recreation
Other changes: language change, keyboard attach/detach, window resize (multi-window mode)
By default, Android destroys and recreates the Activity on every configuration change
Use ViewModel to retain data across recreation without re-fetching from network or database
Declare android:configChanges in manifest to handle changes manually (generally not recommended)
Use onSaveInstanceState() for lightweight UI state like selected tab, scroll position, or typed text
Fragments with setRetainInstance(true) is deprecated — use ViewModel instead

rows.description: Default. A new instance is created every time the Activity is launched, even if one already exists
rows.use_case: Most Activities — email compose, product detail screen

rows.description: Reuses the existing instance if it is already at the top of the back stack; calls onNewIntent()
Only one instance exists in the entire system; brings it to front and clears tasks above it
rows.use_case: Main/home screen of the app, browser-like apps

rows.description: Like singleTask but the Activity occupies its own separate task entirely
rows.use_case: Standalone activities like a dialer or alarm screen

sections.heading: The Back Stack
items: Android maintains a back stack (Last In, First Out) of Activities for each task
When you start a new Activity, it is pushed onto the top of the back stack
When the user presses Back, the current Activity is popped off and destroyed
The previous Activity is resumed from its onStop() state (onRestart → onStart → onResume)
finish() can be called programmatically to remove the current Activity from the stack
Use FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TOP or FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK in Intents to control stack behavior
The Jetpack Navigation Component manages the back stack automatically for Fragment-based apps

rows.scenario: User returns to the app from Home

rows.callbacks_called: onPause → onStop → onDestroy → onCreate → onStart → onResume
onPause → onStop → (process killed, no onDestroy guaranteed)

rows.scenario: Dialog appears on top of Activity
rows.callbacks_called: onPause (Activity partially visible, does NOT call onStop)

sections.heading: Using Lifecycle-Aware Components
items: Lifecycle-aware components from Jetpack observe the Activity lifecycle automatically
Implement LifecycleObserver (or DefaultLifecycleObserver) to tie custom logic to lifecycle events
LiveData is lifecycle-aware — it only delivers updates when the Activity is in STARTED or RESUMED state
ViewModel is scoped to the Activity lifecycle and survives configuration changes
Use lifecycleScope.launch {} to run coroutines that are automatically cancelled on Activity destroy
Use repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED) to collect Flows safely in Activities
ProcessLifecycleOwner tracks the lifecycle of the entire app process (foreground vs background)

rows.fragment: Represents a portion of a screen inside an Activity

rows.activity: Managed by the system's Activity task stack
rows.fragment: Managed by the FragmentManager back stack
Also recreated, but ViewModel retains data

rows.activity: Use as a container; keep logic minimal
rows.fragment: Put most UI logic here for modularity and reuse

sections.heading: Best Practices for Activity Lifecycle
items: Keep onCreate() focused on initialization only — avoid doing heavy work here
Always release resources in the matching opposite callback (acquire in onResume, release in onPause)
Use ViewModel + LiveData/StateFlow to separate UI state from lifecycle callbacks
Never perform long-running operations directly in lifecycle callbacks — use coroutines or WorkManager
Test lifecycle behavior by rotating the screen, backgrounding the app, and using 'Don't keep activities' in Developer Options
Use lifecycleScope and repeatOnLifecycle for safe coroutine and Flow collection
Avoid storing Context in ViewModel — it can cause memory leaks

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: The Activity lifecycle is fundamental to Android development. Every callback exists for a reason, and using them correctly ensures your app is stable, memory-efficient, and provides a smooth user experience. Always pair resource acquisition with proper release, use ViewModel for data survival across rotations, and leverage Jetpack's lifecycle-aware components to write clean, reactive code.
